alkahest 0.0.0.9000
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Smooth

Source: R/AllGenerics.R, R/smooth.R
smooth.Rd

Smoothes intensities.

Usage

smooth_rectangular(x, y, ...)

smooth_triangular(x, y, ...)

smooth_loess(x, y, ...)

smooth_savitzky(x, y, ...)

# S4 method for numeric,numeric
smooth_rectangular(x, y, m = 3)

# S4 method for ANY,missing
smooth_rectangular(x, m)

# S4 method for numeric,numeric
smooth_triangular(x, y, m = 3)

# S4 method for ANY,missing
smooth_triangular(x, m)

# S4 method for numeric,numeric
smooth_loess(x, y, span = 0.75)

# S4 method for ANY,missing
smooth_loess(x, span = 0.75)

# S4 method for numeric,numeric
smooth_savitzky(x, y, m = 3, p = 2)

# S4 method for ANY,missing
smooth_savitzky(x, m, p)

Arguments

x, y

A numeric vector. If y is missing, an attempt is made to interpret x in a suitable way (see grDevices::xy.coords()).

...

Currently not used.

m

An odd integer giving the number of adjacent points to be used.

span

An integer specifying the degree of smoothing (see stats::loess()).

p

An integer giving the degree of the polynomial to be used.

Value

Returns a list with two components x and y.

Details

The following smoothing methods are available:

rectangular

Unweighted sliding-average or rectangular smooth. It replaces each point in the signal with the average of \(m\) adjacent points.

triangular

Weighted sliding-average or triangular smooth. It replaces each point in the signal with the weighted mean of \(m\) adjacent points.

loess

Smooth by Loess fitting.

savitzky

Savitzky-Golay filter. This method is based on the least-squares fitting of polynomials to segments of \(m\) adjacent points.

There will be \((m - 1) / 2\) points both at the beginning and at the end of the spectrum for which a complete \(m\)-width smooth cannot be calculated. To prevent data loss, progressively smaller smooths are used at the ends of the spectrum by smooth_rectangular() and smooth_triangular(). If the Savitzky-Golay filter is used, the original \((m - 1) / 2\) points at the ends of the spectrum are preserved.

References

Gorry, P. A. (1990). General Least-Squares Smoothing and Differentiation by the Convolution (Savitzky-Golay) Method. Analytical Chemistry, 62(6), p. 570-573. doi:10.1021/ac00205a007 .

Savitzky, A. & Golay, M. J. E. (1964). Smoothing and Differentiation of Data by Simplified Least Squares Procedures. Analytical Chemistry, 36(8), p. 1627-1639. doi:10.1021/ac60214a047 .

See also

Other signal processing methods: rescale, signal_transform(), subset()

Author

N. Frerebeau

Examples

## gamma-ray spectrometry
data("LaBr")

## Plot spectrum
LaBr <- signal_select(LaBr, from = 1350, to = 1650)
plot(LaBr, type = "l", xlab = "Energy (keV)", ylab = "Count")


## Rectangular smoothing
LaBr_unweighted <- smooth_rectangular(LaBr, m = 3)
plot(LaBr_unweighted, type = "l", xlab = "Energy (keV)", ylab = "Count")


## Triangular smoothing
LaBr_weighted <- smooth_triangular(LaBr, m = 5)
plot(LaBr_weighted, type = "l", xlab = "Energy (keV)", ylab = "Count")


## Loess smoothing
LaBr_loess <- smooth_loess(LaBr, span = 0.75)
plot(LaBr_loess, type = "l", xlab = "Energy (keV)", ylab = "Count")


## Savitzky–Golay filter
LaBr_savitzky <- smooth_savitzky(LaBr, m = 21, p = 2)
plot(LaBr_savitzky, type = "l", xlab = "Energy (keV)", ylab = "Count")

On this page

alkahest is a part of the tesselle project, a collection of packages for research and teaching in archaeology. Learn more at tesselle.org.

Developed by Nicolas Frerebeau.

Site built with pkgdown 2.0.6.9000.
